title: fix(repostore): prevent underflow when deleting datasets
body:
Storing a manifest does not increase the refCount. So, when deleting a manifest, the refCount starts at zero, and was always being decremented, for both manifests and blocks. This PR adds a conditional check to only decrement the refCount when it's a block.

It also adds `max(0, ...)` calculations to `Natural` assignments to ensure no underflows happen.

title: [BUG] Connect API does not work using the peerId only
body:
## Bug Description
The `connect` API fails to establish connections between non-bootstrap nodes when using only the peer ID, even though all nodes are connected to the same bootstrap node.

## Environment
- Number of nodes: 3
- Node 1: Bootstrap node
- Node 2 & 3: Regular nodes using Node 1's SPR as `bootstrap-node` option

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start Node 1 as the bootstrap node
2. Start Node 2 and Node 3, both configured with Node 1's SPR as the `bootstrap-node` parameter
3. Retrieve the peer ID from Node 2
4. From Node 3, attempt to connect to Node 2 using only the peer ID (without providing `addrs`)

## Expected Behavior
The connection should succeed automatically. Since both nodes share the same bootstrap node, peer discovery should allow Node 3 to resolve Node 2's addresses and establish the connection.

## Actual Behavior
The connection fails with one of these errors:
```
ERR 2026-02-09 09:45:40.711+04:00 Failed to CONNECT.
topics="libstorage libstoragep2p" tid=288554 
error="Failed to connect to peer: Unable to establish outgoing link in internalConnect"
```

or
```
Unable to dial peer
```

**Environment:**
 - OS: Fedora
 - Version: 43
 - Tag/Commit: a5ce5e1fb61329884769fc100fb731a913b97dac
 - Arch: amd64

**Additional context**
- The issue occurs specifically when connecting using peer ID alone
- Both nodes are successfully connected to the bootstrap node

title: new NAT thread management
body:
Today I decided to dive in into `libcodex`. On my two machines (`AMD RYZEN AI MAX+ 395 (32)` and `AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 (8+16)`), the library crashes with segmentation fault when trying to create a second codex node in the same process (even when the first node was already stopped and destroyed successfully). It worked on Arnaud's machine and works on CI, thus we somehow blamed my setup...

Initially I hoped that maybe just building it nativaly will fix the problem.
So, of course, I could not compile it as it seems that `nim-secp256k1` has problem with some modern AMD RYZEN machines. The fix that seem to work was changing in `vendor/nim-codex/vendor/nim-secp256k1/secp256k1/abi.nim`:

```nim
-  const asmFlags = " -DUSE_ASM_X86_64"
+  # Disable x86_64 inline assembly on modern AMD (Zen 4+/Ryzen AI)
+  const asmFlags = " -DUSE_SCALAR_GENERIC"
```

Regarding the segmentation fault, I had some iterations with gpt-5-codex and here is an edited summary:

- The crash happens immediately after `Switch.start()` completes in `vendor/nim-codex/codex/codex.nim` (line `238`), before any of the block‑exchange or discovery logs appear. The next code that runs is `nattedAddress(...)`, so the blow‑up is occurring while we compute NAT mappings, not inside libp2p.
- `vendor/nim-codex/codex/nat.nim` keeps all runtime state in module‑level globals (`strategy`, `extIp`, `natClosed`, `activeMappings`, `natThreads`) plus a single `addQuitProc(stopNatThreads)` hook. That design only works when one node lives in the process. As soon as you create a second node, both instances mutate the same sequences and atomics:
	- `redirectPorts` appends to the shared `activeMappings/natThreads` and spawns a renewal thread for every caller. Those renewal threads and any later redirectPorts invocations then race over the same global state.
	- When the first node shuts down (or when the quit proc runs) `stopNatThreads()` sets the shared `natClosed` atomic to `true`, tears down all threads and deletes every mapping in `activeMappings`, regardless of which node created them. The other live node continues executing with stale NAT state and dangling C pointers, which is consistent with the `SIGSEGV` you’re observing when the second node starts up.
	- Because the globals are never re‑initialised (`natClosed` never flips back to `false`, `activeMappings` is never cleared safely), the second node inherits half torn‑down structures. That’s why the bug only shows up once you create another node in the same process.
- The behaviour matches your platform reports: on Ubuntu (CI) we don’t hit the race because the NAT code exits early (CI runs in environments without UPnP/NAT‑PMP), whereas on your AMD desktops the NAT calls succeed, meaning we actually enter the broken multi‑instance path and crash.
- Also notice that the crash only happens once we actually go down the NAT path far enough to spin up per-process state and threads. Arnaud's Fedora does not suffer from the crash (regardless if he uses Nix or without Nix - I am **not** using Nix BTW), but that's can also be explained: if his Fedora is missing/blocked UPnP/NAT-PMP support, or the router refuses those protocols, `getExternalIP()` simply returns `none`. In that case `redirectPorts` and the renewal threads never run, so the global state never gets into a bad cross-node mix — the code just keeps the default `nat=any` without doing any mapping, and everything appears fine. On my AMD boxes the call likely succeeds (UPnP works), so the brittle shared state is exercised and I am hitting the segfault.

To make multiple nodes safe, we need to turn NAT management into a per-node resource.

The code on this branch is mostly authored by "gpt-5-codex" and so, it is just an entry point to actual work. The initial fix compiles and appears to work, but we had to temporarily disable the NAT port‑mapping renewal thread so we no longer spawn a background thread with `repeatPortMapping`. This cannot go to production like this as NAT will not be able to adjust to changing conditions, but, I think it is a great start.

With this initial fix, I can at least start two nodes one after another with libcodex and even two of them at the same time, even with NAT set to `any`. Also apparently it fixed some logging issues that I have observed - for some combination of log levels for the codex nodes in one process, the node that stops second could in some circumstances hang on `stop` and `destroy` operations. With this initial fix, it seems not to be a problem any more.

To experiments with libcodex, and to experience the problem yourself, you may like to check the following two repos:

- https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-go-bindings
- https://github.com/codex-storage/go-codex-client (can be good for isolated experiments and test runs)

title: Giving Nimble another try
body:
With the recent changes, Codex is now going to be integrated into Status and Waku, rather than running as a standalone application, at least for now.

I think it makes sense to try once again to move to Nimble. The main benefits are:

* Easier integration for other projects in IFT that want to use Codex

* Better alignment with the Nim ecosystem

* Potential Nim tooling support if we manage to split the project into separate modules

There’s some hope this time. Nimble has improved since I last tried it (about a year ago), and it looks like Durability Labs managed to make it work.

Note: Waku tend to follow [this approach](https://github.com/status-im/status-go/issues/6970).

title: Sentry integration
body:
I recently discovered that Sentry offers a special plan for open-source projects: https://sentry.io/for/open-source.

Sentry provides error tracking with full context, including user data, environment separation (devnet, testnet), versioning support...

Although there’s no official Nim SDK yet, we can still use the Sentry HTTP API to report errors manually.

Why does this matter?

Because we need a reliable way to monitor and analyze errors occurring in Codex nodes.

title: Basic NAT traversal components integration
body:
Once we have a decision mechanism in place around whether we are reachable or not, and basic options are depleted (eq., our current UPnP implementation), we should incorporate other techniques like hole punching and relaying in order to achieve the node's connectivity.

In this step we should then integrate:
 - `HPService`
 - `AutoRelayService`
 - [Update Discv5 announced addresses](https://github.com/waku-org/nwaku/blob/master/waku/factory/waku.nim#L95)

title: DHT Client mode
body: Because the NAT traversal techniques will be implemented on the libp2p level, the Discv5 DHT does not have NAT traversal capability. In this way the nodes behind NAT will not be able to route traffic as part of the DHT and hence we should introduce a mode of DHT operation that will reflect this. This will also help with #1320 as the random DHT walk will introduce publicly reachable nodes which most probably will also support Relay protocol.

title: Extend `AutoRelayService` to populate peers from Discv5
body:
The `AutoRelayService` performs relay discovery using the node's peerstore by searching for a peer that supports the Relay protocol. But because we use libp2p only for the dataset transfers, specifically, we do not use libp2p's DHT. That means that the libp2p's peerstore will not be really populated.

We need to extend the behavior of the `AutoRelayService` to incorporate the population of the peerstore from Discv5 DHT. The current idea is to periodically populate the peer store with a random DHT traversal.

title: NAT Reachability indicators
body: Create a basic structure for NAT traversal and create a decision component that will guide the utilization of NAT traversal techniques. This component will utilize AutoNAT to get reachability indicators from it, and preferably, should be enough to guide these decisions and not require any configuration from the user themselves.

title: Handling of several active smart contracts
body:
As part of the [smart contract deployment and upgrade strategy](https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-research/blob/master/design/contract-deployment.md), there will be two types of upgrades - feature and emergency upgrades.

In the case of feature upgrades, we envision that new smart contract will be deployed and the old ones will be deprecated. But still for some time the nodes might need to honor their commitments from the old contracts, so we need to be able to support several versions of smart contracts for some time.

title: `--network` flag support
body:
I would like to propose a new CLI flag/configuration `--network=[devnet|testnet]`. This is in order to give users sensible default settings and easy switching between **well-known** networks we maintain. 

I don't propose removing any of the existing flags, which would prevent from easily joining different (for example private) networks, so no functionality will be lost, I just want to make life easier.

This flag would work in a simple way, where its usage would configure preconfigured SPR and smart-contract addresses, hence joining the desired network. We already have the "auto-discovery" of smart contract addresses based on the chain ID of the connected provider, so this mechanism would allow two new use-cases:

1. No need to manually specify SPR records ==> simplification
2. The "auto-discovery" smart contract addresses based on Chain ID has the limitation of one network per Chain ID, where with our Devnet being created, there will be two networks on one chain, hence making this mechanism non-functional.

title: [BUG] Manifest-block expiry not updated
body:
Seen in testnet at v0.2.5

The /data endpoints of our cloud nodes reports that no datasets are stored, while the nodes are actively hosting and proving slots.

This is the result of not updating that manifest-block expiry. There are two implementations of `updateExpiry` in node.nim. In both cases the manifest block is missed. As a result, after the default TTL the manifest block is deleted by maintenance while the data blocks remain.

The manifest block is required. Once the manifest's DHT entries time out, the data can no longer be retrieved.

title: [BUG] Codex attempts to download circuit, even when they are already locally available
body: When starting codex, with contract autodiscovery, it appears to always attempt to download the circuit files, even when already available locally, this in turn blocks codex until it is able to retrieve the files. This isn't the expected behavior, instead, we should only attempt to download circuit files only when they aren't already present, in addition to that, codex should remain responsive - e.g. able to process HTTP requests.

title: [BUG] Disk full of orphaned blocks
body:
Seen in the v0.2.3 testnet.

Cloud node 03 and 04 are logging:
`LevelDbDatastore.put exception: IO error: /data/meta/089621.ldb: No space left on device`

The cloud nodes each have 100GB of space allocated to them.

The `/data` endpoint shows 3 datasets present. Their total size: 987430912 => 0.9GB
The `/space` endpoint shows:
```json
{
	"totalBlocks": 1530468,
	"quotaMaxBytes": 105226698752,
	"quotaUsedBytes": 100272881146,
	"quotaReservedBytes": 4928552960
}
```

"quotaUsedBytes": 100272881146 => 93.4 GB

The disk is full of blocks that do not belong to a dataset: Orphaned blocks.

Maintenance module is in charge of cleaning up these blocks. It appears to be running, but unable to delete the blocks. Logs reveal many occurances of:
```
TRC 2025-06-09 09:30:32.428+00:00 Attempting to delete a block               topics="codex repostore" tid=1 cid=zDx*6GeRLL count=30577438
TRC 2025-06-09 09:30:32.428+00:00 Unable to delete block from repoStore      tid=1 count=30577439
```

To fix:
Maintenance module code does not show the error message. So we don't know why it can't delete the blocks.
Maintenance logging doesn't use a log topic.
Timer util used by maintenance module catches CancelledError, but (maybe?) silently swallows any other errors.

title: [BUG] Discovery request queue is full
body:
Storage nodes in the testnet have began logging
`Exception queueing discovery request       topics="codex discoveryengine" tid=1 exc="AsyncQueue is full!"`
Occurance is increasing over time.

This is very probably causing failure to discover blocks -> download slots -> start contracts.

I suspected the discovery worker had stopped processing the queue. But,
`trace "Discovery task cancelled"`
`info "Exiting discovery task runner"`
do not appear in the logs.

However, looking at `proc discoveryTaskLoop` it seems possible for the worker loop to be stopped *without leaving any log entry* by any exception that is not a CancelledError. (Should this be impossible due to compiler-checked exceptions?)

Can we confirm somehow whether the discovery worker has been stopped? (There should be 10 of them by default.) Or is there a likely explanation for why the workers can't keep up with the queue?

title: [BUG] Reserved quota locks up storage nodes
body:
Seen in testnet of v0.2.3.

After several days of successful running, the testnet has stopped filling new contracts. Examining the trace of a storage contract and logs from the nodes involved, we find this:
- The nodes successfully reserved a slot
- The nodes begin downloading
- The download failed: Storing the block would exceed the quota limit.

Typical quota values logged by this error message:
```
Used:        33387095674 (31GB)
reserved:    71839629312 (67GB)
limit:      105226698752 (98GB)
```

This shows that roughly 2/3 of the capacity of the node is used by reservations. This is true at a moment where the testnet is "quiet": There are no new/pending storage contracts.

This suggest that there is a possible flow of the sales module that does not properly clean up reservations.

title: feat: network retry implementation
feat: retry with exponential backoff
body:
Network-related errors, such as connection timeouts, should be bubbled up to nim-codex, where they can be caught and a request retry attempted repeatedly until a threshold of retries is reached. If the threshold is reached, another exception indicating a network failure should be raised and then handled accordingly.

Network-related errors to handle (with their corresponding `nim-json-rpc` error types):
- connection timed out (`RpcPostError`)
- connection closed during response (`FailedHttpResponse`)
- HTTP 429 "too many requests" (`ErrorResponse(status=429)`)

### `nim-ethers` changes
Currently, `nim-ethers` [converts all `nim-json-rpc` exceptions into `JsonRpcProviderError`](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-ethers/blob/ec7d415db6163441e5e9e90e0faa0dbc23fffab3/ethers/providers/jsonrpc/errors.nim#L45-L46). This should be modified to convert them into an ethers-specific exception type, eg `EthersNetworkError`. The other option is to bubble up the errors as is but this will create a double-handling situation, where those specific errors need to be treated differently at both the ethers level and the codex level.


### `nim-codex` changes
There are several places in the sales state machine in particular that require retry functionality to prevent side effects like node crashes and potential loss of collateral. We tossed up the idea of implementing the retry functionality at the codex layer and the ethers layer, but ultimately decided that this should be abstracted in the codex layer since this functionality in `ethersjs` is only implemented for HTTP 429, and not things like connection timeouts.

The abstraction should probably be Future-specific, instead of being applied to a block of code, such that each call that has a network error is waited on until a retry is successful or ultimately fails. It would also be nice from an api perspective, if this could be chained to a Future (see #1232 for more)

The network retry functionality will then need to be applied to relevant RPC-related calls, likely most of the contract calls in the market abstraction. This should be adequate enough to cover most of the networking-related failures that would causes. In particular we need to ensure we cover:
1. The issue in `SalePreparing` (#1215), as well as other states that would raise a Defect if prior network calls would fail
2. The found issues in the [recoverability analysis](https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-research/blob/refactor/simplified-sales-and-purchasing/analysis/recoverability_analysis.md) where a retry functionality is recommended.

title: Dataset health
body:
# Context
During the all-Codex in Trogir, we discussed how best to support altruistic mode. This task followed from that session. Supporting this feature would allow users to detect the "health" of their datasets in the network. This enables altruistic use-cases to implement automatic mechanisms to organize repair/dispersal of altruistic datasets. We discussed and agreed that the disemination of the altruistic-dataset-CIDs would be handled outside of Codex. This feature would allow the code that controls this to take action when datasets are at risk.

# Tasks
- Understand how to measure the "health" of a dataset. Is it enough to count the nodes who contain the manifest? Should there be some kind of block-sampling? (Keep in mind: We should *NOT* attempt to provide solid/highly reliable durability information or guarantees this way. For use cases that require high durability guarantees we should direct them to use the marketplace.)
- Design the changes necessary to support this feature at the network level
- Design the API to access the dataset health information
- Buidl!

title: Allow erasure-coding through API
body:
# Context
During the all-Codex in Trogir, we discussed how best to support altruistic mode. This task followed from that session. Supporting this feature would allow users to apply erasure-coding to their data, even if they are not going to use the marketplace to purchase storage for it. This would enable network-level reconstruction for datasets of altruistic use-cases.

# Tasks
- Create one or more new API calls that allow the user to create an erasure-coded dataset given the CID of a non-erasure-coded dataset.
- If the user attempts to create an erasure-coded dataset for a dataset that is already erasure-coded, return an error.
Adjust the marketplace purchase API call such that:
- The purchase call no longer accepts the erasure-coding properties
- The purchase call returns an error when called with the CID of a non-erasure-coded dataset.

# Bonus reasons to pick up this work
- This task would allow users to easily purchase storage for already-encoded datasets from previously running contracts. (extending contracts without having to decode/re-encode OR double-encode (current behavior))
- Closes https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/issues/1005

title: Support for Codex on Mobile (light client)
body:
As a client of Codex, I want to run Codex on restricted devices like a mobile phone.

The end goal is for mobile clients to have access to file fetching and file sending without having to support the load of the full network, so this would require to limit bandwidth, RAM, CPU and storage.

The direct use case for my team, Status, is that we would like to use Codex for sending files in our Chat instead of using the Waku Network. We would also like in the future to also support the Codex Market.
For this goal, we need to have Codex work on mobile devices, because it is a major part of our user base.

This will also enable more clients to use Codex, since Mobile phones represent a vast majority of user devices.

title: `mySlots` onchain mapping is not reflected with the agents in Sales state machine
body:
I noticed one thing endpoint `/sales/slots` gives slots, but the endpoint `/sales/slots/<id>` fails to return details about it with the message "Provider not filling slot".
This means that slots are being tracked onchain with the `mySlots` mapping (which is used to return the data with `/sales/slots` endpoint), but the host does not have active Sales Agent for it running (which is used to serve the `/sales/slots/<id>` endpoint).

title: [BUG] Persistence and the config.toml file
body:
The argument "persistance" and its nested arguments cannot be provided using the toml configuration file.

Expected config file:
```
otherFields="value"
...

[persistence]
eth-provider="http://address"
eth-private-key="eth.key"


```
Persistence and nested arguments are ignored and API calls return "Persistence is not enabled".

Providing persistence via the CLI arguments:
`codex persistance --config-file=config.toml`
Results in node startup-failure with "Unexpected field 'eth-provider' while deserializing".

Probably the "prover" argument can also not be provided with the config file.

title: Sales state machine: retry failed network requests after slot is filled
body: If there are failed network requests after a slot has been filled, we need to ensure that the state machine will not go to `SaleErrored` and prevent recovery without a restart. We should check network-related errors coming from nim-json-rpc into nim-ethers, and bubble those specific networking errors up to codex. These errors can then be handled in a retry with backoff fashion to repeatedly retry until the network-related issue has been resolved

title: Marketplace vault
body:
Uses the latest version of the Marketplace contract that utilize the Vault contract.

This includes the following changes:
- separate reward and collateral addresses are no longer supported

title: [BUG, integration tests] slotSize and datasetSize computation not correct
body:
We use the following two operations to compute `slotSize` and `datasetSize`. This is not entirely correct (I wrote that, btw), and while it gives correct values for the block numbers we use in tests currently, it will not be correct when given odd values for the number of block.

I will fix that soon.

```nim
proc slotSize(blocks, nodes, tolerance: int): UInt256 =
  let ecK = nodes - tolerance
  let blocksRounded = roundUp(blocks, ecK)
  let blocksPerSlot = divUp(blocksRounded, ecK)
  (DefaultBlockSize * blocksPerSlot.NBytes).Natural.u256

proc datasetSize(blocks, nodes, tolerance: int): UInt256 =
  return nodes.u256 * slotSize(blocks, nodes, tolerance)
```

title: [BUG] Error during `downloading` state might lead to "overflow" of availability's size
body:
The bug is related to quite an edge-case, but IMHO still valid. It is related to the fact that reservations.deleteReservation() call will return "undownloaded" bytes to the availability. The edge-case bug will be present if there will be an error during the downloading state which will lead to calling cleanUp, where we will return the bytes for the whole slot using the reservations.returnBytesToAvailability(), but then we will also return the remaning "undownloaded" bytes, which could lead to having freeSize > totalSize of availability.

Discovered as part of https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/pull/1099#pullrequestreview-2697723125

This way ignoring some "troublesome" blocks would be easier, without going through the store/delete cycle.

title: Repo Store Benchmarks
body:
## **Benchmark Results Summary**

Here are the benchmarks for basic operations on repoStore (using **levelDB** for metaDS and **fileDS** for blockDS), as well as high-level APIs like `store`, `retrieve`, and `delete`.

### **Small Operations (500 iterations)**

| Operation | blst_sha256 | nimcrypto_sha256 | Difference |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| get_cid_and_proof | 0.00016s | 0.00016s | No difference |
| del_block | 0.00020s | 0.00020s | No difference |
| has_block | 0.00010s | 0.00010s | No difference |
| del_cid | 0.00007s | 0.00006s | Similar |
| put_cid_and_proof | 0.00026s | 0.00025s | Similar |
| put_block | 0.00023s | 0.00022s | Similar |
| get_block | 0.00156s | 0.01197s | ~7.7x slower |

### **Large Operations (1 iteration)**

| Operation | blst_sha256 | nimcrypto_sha256 | Difference |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| store | 51.15s | 62.01s | ~21% slower |
| retrieve_entire_manifest | 13.52s | 23.87s | ~76% slower |
| delete_entire_manifest | 16.83s | 18.15s | ~8% slower |

## **Key Findings**

1. Small Operations:
    - Most small operations show similar performance between both implementations
    - Significant difference in `get_block` operation where nimcrypto_sha256 is ~7.7x slower
2. Large Operations:
    - blst_sha256 consistently outperforms nimcrypto_sha256
    - Most notable difference in retrieve_entire_manifest (76% performance gap)
    - Store operation shows 21% better performance with blst_sha256
    - Delete operation shows minimal difference (8%)

## **Recommendations**

Based on these benchmarks, blst_sha256 implementation shows superior performance, particularly for:

- Block retrieval operations
- Large-scale manifest operations
- Store operations

Consider standardizing on blst_sha256 for better overall performance, especially in scenarios involving frequent block retrievals and manifest operations.

title: Partitioned quotas for repostore
body:
Following up on #1142, we got to the realization that the current scheme for tracking reservations at the RepoStore is not working well ([report here](https://hackmd.io/lWcfzetlQ0CR0gI-QOvtSw)).

After discussion with @markspanbroek and @dryajov, we came to the conclusion that supporting reservations at the RepoStore level; i.e., the ability to partition the main quota into identified subquotas, is probably easier to get right. This issue tracks that feature.

title: Testnet with realistic ZK circuit
body:
Currently on testnet, we're using a ZK circuit that only checks 5 samples per storage proof. This is too little for proper detection of missing data. A value of 50 or 100 is more realistic. This impacts the size of the circuit artifacts and the prover time, so it's likely that this leads to different timing. Therefore we should update the testnet to work with a higher number of samples.

Discord discussion with more background:
https://discord.com/channels/895609329053474826/1230457525854539819/1348962165138259968

title: Freeze guard
[BUG] Updating totalCollateral resets totalRemainingCollateral without considering previous reservations
body:
When an availability is created or updated, the totalRemainingCollateral is set to totalCollateral. However, if a reservation was created before the availability is updated, the collateral subtracted when creating the reservation will not be reflected in the new totalRemainingCollateral.

Here is a nim test for the details:

```nim
  test "updating totalCollateral takes in consideration the previous reservations":
    var availability = createAvailability()
    # Make sure that the totalCollateral is equal to the totalRemainingCollateral when the 
    # availability is created
    check availability.totalCollateral == availability.totalRemainingCollateral

    let collateralPerByte = 1.u256
    let reservation = createReservation(availability)
    let collateral = reservation.size.u256 * collateralPerByte

    let key = key(availability.id).get
    var res = await reservations.get(key, Availability)
    check isOk res
    var updated = res.get    

    # The totalRemainingCollateral is now equal to the totalCollateral
    # minus the reservation size * collateral
    check updated.totalRemainingCollateral == updated.totalCollateral - collateral

    # Increase the total collateral of the availability
    availability.totalCollateral = availability.totalCollateral + 100.u256
    check isOk await reservations.update(availability)

    res = await reservations.get(key, Availability)
    check isOk res
    updated = res.get    

    # The new totalRemainingCollateral should be equal to the totalCollateral 
    # minus the previous collateral removed from the reservation
    check updated.totalRemainingCollateral == updated.totalCollateral - collateral
```

title: Bittorrent Integration [draft]
body:
Draft of the BitTorrent Codex integration.

Should be enough to see the overall idea in code.

What is there:

- `storeTorrent` is equivalent of Codex `store` - this is the entry point when seeding BitTorrent content into Codex network
- `retrieveTorrent` is equivalent of Codex `retrieve` - entry point when requesting BitTorrent content having only `info` hash as the input.

To be functional still needs:

- [x] b-encoding and the actual info-hash validation (now there is only a placeholder around the place where it most probably will  happen)
- [x] streaming pieces and validating them
- [x] REST API (download)
- [x] REST API (upload)
- [x] REST API (fetch torrent manifest only for the given `info` hash)
- [x] Try to make the Cid for BitTorrent Manifest a valid Cid - investigation done - not technically feasible and does not add any more security.
- [x] update docs
- [x] a bit more testing

See also https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-pm/issues/211.

body:
After all the bytes have been uploaded, the node will create the merkle tree. On large datasets, this interrupts the main loop long enough for the node to be dropped by its peers.

`without tree =? CodexTree.init(cids), err:`

title: Rolling logfiles
body:
Currently:
`--log-file=<file>`
File is created and overwritten each time Codex starts. Log of previous run is destroyed.
File name has no date/time information.
File doesn't roll over.
(File also seems to have strange access rules: Can't read or tail file while Codex is running. Not convenient.)

Requested upgrade:
Instead of a log file, config points to a log folder.
Codex creates timestamped log files in the log folder.
Each file is used for a maximum amount of time. (24h? 1w? configurable?)
Old log files are automatically deleted after a fix amount of time. (3 months?)

title: Deletes are SLOW
body: I've added a delete endpoint to Codex in #1103, but deletes are ridiculously slow. It takes 4 minutes to download a 4GB file in my machine. I suspect this is part of a broader set of issues with how we manage data, but if we want to scale to 100GB+ datasets, then this is not acceptable (it'd take 1h40mins to delete a 100GB dataset).

title: feat(integration): Parallelize integration tests
body:
Runs integration tests in parallel, by running each integration test file specified in `testIntegration` in a separate process. Note, this is all still single threaded, using chronos' `AsyncProcess`.

The result is that the integration tests run about 60% faster locally (~26 mins on my machine) than if run serially.
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/678fd183-f672-452a-a1f9-c2117dc8b015)

I will update this description with the results in CI..

## Configuration
There are few options for configuring the `TestManager`, which can be set in the following ways:
1. Set the value when running the `make testIntegration` command, eg:
 ```shell
make DEBUG_CODEXNODES=1 testIntegration
```
2. By defining the value and running `testIntegration` directly.  This option is probably faster assuming that the codex binary does not need to re-built each time. For example:
```shell
./env.sh nim c -d:DebugCodexNodes=true -r tests/testIntegration.nim
```
3. Changing the value(s) directly in `tests/testIntegration.nim`

### Chronicles logs
For chronicles logs 

### Option definitions
Default values indicate what values are set when `make testIntegration` is run.
| Option | Make param | Default | Description |
|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| `debugTestHarness` |  `DEBUG_TESTHARNESS` | `false` | Echoes stderr if there's a test failure (eg test failed, compilation error) or error (eg test manager error) |
| `debugHardhat` | `DEBUG_HARDHAT` | `false` | Echoes stdout from Hardhat process |
| `debugCodexNodes` | `DEBUG_CODEXNODES` | `true` | Echoes stdout from the integration test file process. Codex process logs can also be output if a test uses a multinodesuite, requires CodexConfig.debug to be enabled. Note: default is `true` so that the output of `unittest/unittest2` in stdout will be shown, ie `[Suite] <suite name>` and `[Test] <test name>`, eg ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd7b579a-89f5-46bd-b529-17c572c7f4d2) |
| `showContinuousStatusUpdates` | `DEBUG_UPDATES` | `false` | Shows test status updates at time intervals. Useful for running locally with active terminal interaction. Set to false for unattended runs, eg CI. Example: ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/95ee0c69-b2ed-4cf8-b8b5-5024ff9d8377) |
| `testTimeout` | `TEST_TIMEOUT` | `60.minutes` | Timeout duration (in minutes) for EACH integration test file. |

title: Peer want-list maintenance
body:
A node keeps track of the wants of its peers. Currently this is a simple seq in `peercontext.nim`. A malicious peer could spam entries and break our node.

We should change this by adding the following functionality:
- A max length for peer wantList, settable via conf option, defaulted to 100 (?)
- When trying to add new entries to a full wantList, consider priority and replace low priority with high priority entries.

body:
I was thinking of exposing the whole Marketplace config through the API because it can communicate the properties of the network to the user - for example, how many slashes is needed to vacate the filled slot etc. For sure not everything will be really useful for everybody, but at the same time I don't think there is any "secret" here or something that could go wrong with exposing it. Also, the structure of the config will change only with breaking releases as that will require the contract's redeployment, so IMHO no problem from "stableness of API" POV.

But I noticed a pattern that the Marketplace contract's `config` is not really exposed but rather its specific properties are, which sort of prevents me to do this so I am wondering what is the reasoning for it and can we expose it in its fullness? @markspanbroek  could you give me please some context here?

title: A more generic version of `eventually` in `asynctest`
body:
In #922, a more generic version of `eventually` has been introduced to have a bit more control over awaiting for asynchronous tasks in tests.

The version was called `eventuallyS` where `S` stands for _slow_. It changes the `timeout` argument to use _seconds_ instead of _milliseconds_ (default `10`), introduces `step` argument to adjust the waiting time between testing the condition (default `5`, meaning `5s`) and optional `cancelExpression` to interrupt the waiting loop and return `false` when `cancelExpression` evaluates to `true`:

```nim
template eventuallyS(expression: untyped, timeout=10, step = 5,
    cancelExpression: untyped = false): bool =
  bind Moment, now, seconds

  proc eventuallyS: Future[bool] {.async.} =
    let endTime = Moment.now() + timeout.seconds
    var secondsElapsed = 0
    while not expression:
      if endTime < Moment.now():
        return false
      if cancelExpression:
        return false
      await sleepAsync(step.seconds)
    return true

  await eventuallyS()
```

The intention is to use the above function to replace the original implementation.

> If we stick to `milliseconds` as the unit and keep the default `timeout` value set to `5000` and step being `10` we would get fully backward-compatible version. We could also consider increasing the default step to something more appropriate for (slow) integration tests, e.g. `1000` (meaning we would have `1s` between testing the condition).

Two functions need to be changed in the `asynctest` module:

1. `asynctest/private/chronos/eventually.nim`
2. `asynctest/asynctest/private/asyncdispatch/eventually.nim`

**Estimated cost: 1 (ideal working day)**

title: Handle limited number of entries returned from eth_getLogs RPC call
body:
This is to capture the remark from @AuHau in the context of #922 (direct link to the comment: https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/pull/922#pullrequestreview-2495503006). Here is a summary of the discussion we had with respect to that comment.

With some RPC providers the number of entries returned by the `eth_getLogs` RPC call is limited. For example, [Infura has a limit of 10 000 results coming back from eth_getLogs call](https://docs.metamask.io/services/reference/ethereum/json-rpc-methods/eth_getlogs/#constraints). As a raw estimate, for an average of 4 slots per request, we would hit this limit if our network has an average of  `10000 / 4 / 30 = 83` storage requests per day. Also in the [Logs](https://docs.lineascan.build/sepolia-lineascan/api-endpoints/logs) in the [Linea Scan API documentation](https://docs.lineascan.build) we read that:

> For performance & security considerations, only the first 1000 results are return. So please narrow down the filter parameters.

On their [Network information page](https://docs.linea.build/get-started/build/network-info), Linea recommends using Infura for the RPC URL, which means that the Infura restriction above may quickly become relevant to us.

Our current resolution is to record the issue (this issue), observe the number of storage requests on the network and when approaching the limit, amend the operations accordingly by experimentally finding a safe number of blocks to look back so that the number of log entries does not exceed the above limit and then using multiple `getLogs` calls to retrieve the required history.

title: BlkExc - Improved peer selection
body:
I would like to discuss and design our way to better peer selection in the blockexchange engine. I think we can apply a similar pattern as for IndexingStrategy. We can make an abstraction that hides the peer-selection-strategy from the engine code, so that we can later slot in implementations that optimize for speed vs peer-reliability vs price.

What are the inputs required?
-  Block being downloaded
-  Currently connected peers
Per connected peer we might want to know:
 - Measure of reliability (did they respond to previous messages?)
 - Measure of speed (duration of previous block downloads?)
 - Price for block (can be omitted for now)

The biggest puzzles are acceptable ways of detecting reliability, speed, and deciding how to remember faulty/unreliable peers. At the moment, we just disconnect from them. But if discovery returns them again, we will reconnect.

Please put your insights here.

Finally, it's needed for my benchmarking so now I need it too. 😄

title: Add logging to show transaction ids
body:
It can be difficult to debug issues with ethereum transactions going wrong, because we do not log the transaction id.

Proposal is to add debug logging to `contracts/market.nim` that shows the transaction ids, for example [when sending a storage request](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/d7ae8b734a50144290a5b50ca3a84bcf7753c69a/codex/contracts/market.nim#L102).

Thanks for bringing this up, @veaceslavdoina.

title: Add UDP port check to Codex health checks
feat(integration): add ability to search logs in integration test nodes + slot reservations test
body:
Adds the ability for integration tests to search and assert on the log output.

Adds an integration test that checks for "nonce too high" in the logs (which is the issue plaguing the testnet right now).

title: Manifest versioning
body: Manifests need to be versioned so we can have smoother upgrade paths when rolling out breaking changes (e.g. #865). Ideally we'd have a constant within `Manifest` itself which gets initialized to whatever version we're currently using, but that's not possible with Nim so we need to understand the safest way to implement it.

title: Codex/BitTorrent integration
body:
This issue is a starting place for capturing work discussion and progress on integrating Codex with BitTorrent client.
The objective is to gather data and do some experiments allowing us to feel better amount of work required to get Codex client
and BitTorrent working together. Ideally we want to have some tangible result before Devcon.

Ideally, we want to be able to keep as much as possible of the Codex protocol intact while taking advantage of usability feature
and management facilities (downloading, pausing, resuming, etc) that BitTorrent currently provides.

> This issue is related to #951 which to my best understanding focuses on extracting BitTorrent performance metrics.

The work performed by @gmega will be a very good start for this issue:
- [guide on how to setup Deluge to run from sources](https://hackmd.io/ESDTgprbSPmViMxc5yKTiQ)
- [controlling Deluge using its RPC interface](https://github.com/gmega/bt-experiment/blob/c6af36b349f0211df69781233d387de229d68f62/experiment.py#L91)

More info will follow...

title: Use XDG Base Directory
body:
Codex hardcodes default paths to live in `/home/runner`.

```
Available sub-commands:

codex-v0.1 persistence prover [OPTIONS]...

The following options are available:

 -cd, --circuit-dir          Directory where Codex will store proof circuit data
                            [=/home/runner/.cache/codex/circuits].
     --circom-r1cs          The r1cs file for the storage circuit
                            [=/home/runner/.cache/codex/circuits/proof_main.r1cs].
     --circom-wasm          The wasm file for the storage circuit
                            [=/home/runner/.cache/codex/circuits/proof_main.wasm].
     --circom-zkey          The zkey file for the storage circuit
                            [=/home/runner/.cache/codex/circuits/proof_main.zkey].
     --circom-no-zkey       Ignore the zkey file - use only for testing! [=false].
     --proof-samples        Number of samples to prove [=5].
     --max-slot-depth       The maximum depth of the slot tree [=32].
     --max-dataset-depth    The maximum depth of the dataset tree [=8].
     --max-block-depth      The maximum depth of the network block merkle tree [=5].
     --max-cell-elements    The maximum number of elements in a cell [=67].
```

Implement `XDG` Base Directory structure, specifically:

- `XDG_DATA_HOME`
- `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`
- `XDG_CACHE_HOME`

https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir-spec/latest/

title: Add ability to specify announced address(es)
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
In `nwaku` we have these config options:

```
 --ext-multiaddr           External multiaddresses to advertise to the network. Argument may be repeated..
 --ext-multiaddr-only      Only announce external multiaddresses [=false].
```

It is especially useful if you run Kubernetes environment, but might also be useful when using any kind of port-forwarding where extrnal port may not match the listen port

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Allow specifying `listenAddress` and `externalAddress` separately - it is then up to the operator to make sure the connections coming to `externalAddress` are actually tunneled to `listenAddress`

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

We have not found any other working solution in `nwaku` for this particular issue

**Additional context**

Original issue from nwaku: https://github.com/waku-org/nwaku/issues/1797

title: Support arm64 on Windows
body: We currently do not support arm64 on Windows, and the amd64 version apparently doesn't seem to run properly under Prism, so we need to either fix that or release an arm build (preferred).

title: fix: remove `withValue` semaphore and replace with refcount tracking
body:
Remove the semaphore in `withValue`. `withValue` needs manual refcount tracking with async due to raw pointers.

Note that the semaphore was created as a fix to https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/issues/162.

See https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/issues/914 and https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/pull/937#discussion_r1786770308 for context.

title: feat: support collateral top-ups
body:
Related to https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-contracts-eth/issues/187.

During a started request, SPs may be slashed if they miss enough proofs. Currently, there is no way for them to top up their collateral so they are in a "full collateral" state. This weakens the security of the protocol.

Support collateral top ups through the REST API. Add an endpoint that tops up collateral via the contract (described in https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-contracts-eth/issues/187).

title: Expose Request's information for Reservation endpoint
body: Currently, there is RequestId only which is not possible to query against anywhere else on API

title: REST API to fetch manifest
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
I'd like to altruistically cache data from different Codex nodes upon notification (e.g. a Waku message containing a CID). At the same time, I need to make sure that the data I am pulling is not too large - i.e. I want to limit the datasize to .e.g 5MB, but someone can easily send a CID pointing to blob of 50GB and fill my node. 

At the moment the REST API only allows me to directly request data download

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I'd like a REST API endpoint - e.g.

```
http://localhost:8080/api/codex/v1/manifest/{cid}/network
```

Which would only return the manifest obtained from the network for a given CID upon which I could analyze it and call the `data/{cid}/network` endpoint to actually pull the data.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

I guess bindings to other languages would solve this without exposing it through REST API, but since it seems REST is the way to control the node at the moment, it seems to be the best choice.

title: Building Codex on FreeBSD
body:
A pull request to encapsulate what I learned building Codex for freebsd-14.1 (amd64).  

I wouldn't recommend considering merging this request as-is, as A) this is my first experience at patching Nim/Nimbus codebases, and B) the actual changes have more to do with patching codex dependencies than modifying Codex itself.  

This pull request contains:

1.  Documentation of necessary additional FreeBSD system packages in BUILDING.md
2.  Modification to linking to overcome what I think is a problem in the `circom-compat-ffi` Rust crate in failing to include `/usr/lib/libutil.a` for its usage of `kinfo_*` interfaces
3.  A patch to the Rust wasmer-vm-2.3.0 dependency for amd64 architecture (an obvious mistake in the Rust source that is fixed in subsequent releases.

I would be happy for feedback on how to fix the problems in a smoother manner.

title: Data persistence for API
body:
It's not the greatest title but I can't figure something better.

Currently, when you query endpoints like `/slots` or `/purchases` they are backed by the Marketplace's state-machines, and state-machines are loaded upon node's start from on-chain using the contract tracking `mySlots()` and `myRequests()`.

This mechanism does not really keep much of the data it represents around, mainly the Sale's state-machine, because it cleans up after it finishes: https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/e6a387e8e81824b2fcd816f7bde25699bbe1a162/codex/sales.nim#L163

So basically once Sale's state-machine reaches `Finished`/`Cancelled`/`Failed`, it will be right away removed and on API-level you will never be able to actually figure out what happened to the slots you were hosting.

The purchasing machine I believe does not clean up so the "data" will stay there until the node shutdown, but then when it re-starts it won't "reload it" from the `myRequests()` because it is already gone.

This is not great API experience. Most probably for example UI will want to be able fetch history of purchases/sales to display to the user...

title: OpenAPI versioning and exposing it on API
body:
Since we are starting to have apps built on top of the node's API, we should start proper versioning of our API using the OpenAPI's `version` property: https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/master/openapi.yaml#L4

Also, we should expose this version as part of our API, so the client's libraries can check the version of the API that they run against. This is for example important with the OpenAPI generated client libraries.

title: Richer dataset manifests
Calling `ensureExpiry` halfway through a maintenance cycle could result in dataset inconsistencies
body:
We are currently rather lax with dataset-level operations being allowed to run together with the GC (dataset maintenance). There is at least one case where we have identified this to be an issue:

1. GC is running for a given dataset;
2. we try to extend the lifespan of a slot after reservation.

At that point you may have already purged blocks from disk and end up with an incomplete dataset. The fix for this specific issue is that we should not allow extensions to happen as GC is running. In broader terms, I'd argue that any operations that might affect a dataset (as identified by its `treeCid`) should probably be reordered (or outright denied) until after the GC is done.

title: Issuing more than 10 concurrent downloads can cause them to fail
body:
When running multiple parallel downloads (e.g. $\geq 10$), downloads will oftentimes hang. Logs show:

```text
WRN 2024-07-08 12:09:56.756+02:00 Exception queueing discovery request       topics="codex discoveryengine" tid=34351074 exc="AsyncQueue is full!"
WRN 2024-07-08 12:09:56.757+02:00 Exception queueing discovery request       topics="codex discoveryengine" tid=34351074 exc="AsyncQueue is full!"
WRN 2024-07-08 12:09:56.757+02:00 Exception queueing discovery request       topics="codex discoveryengine" tid=34351074 exc="AsyncQueue is full!"
```

To reproduce this, you can:

1. upload about $40$ $1\text{MB}$ files into the testnet;
2. run a local client and issue $40$ parallel downloads over the REST API.

The issue seems to be [lack of backpressure](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/3ae73197c2cb829607678f64832500a66a26bac0/codex/blockexchange/engine/discovery.nim#L194C33-L194C34) on the request queue to the discovery service. Note that the same seems to happen when [advertising provider records](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/3ae73197c2cb829607678f64832500a66a26bac0/codex/blockexchange/engine/discovery.nim#L206), and may lead to more puzzling failures (i.e. you may end up getting publish failures which later cause lookup failures).

This is a particular issue for app/UI workloads which may attempt to download several files simultaneously. Fixing this requires a review of the request flow to discovery, and the addition of backpressure at the correct locations.

title: [BUG] Using range types can crash a program with `SIGSEGV`
body:
**Description**
Range types such as `Natural` have several fundamental issues and we should probably not use them in our code base. Some reference https://status-im.github.io/nim-style-guide/language.range.html

To represent subset of integer types we can consider defining our own type, that's not a range type:
```nim
type CodexNatural = disctinct int
```

along with accompanying conversion methods, which throw `CatchableError` instead of `IndexDefect`
```nim
12.CodexNatural
-1.CodexNatural # throws

# or alternatively
CodexNatural.init(12)
CodexNatural.init(-1) # throws
```

**To Reproduce**
This test ends with `SIGSEGV: Illegal storage access. (Attempt to read from nil?)` ran on nim-codex version `v0.1.2`. It won't crash if we use `T = int`

```nim
test "Should not crash but it will":
  type T = Natural

  let
    iter = Iter.new(0..<5)
      .map((i: int) => i.T)
      .filter((i: T) => (i mod 2) == 1)

  check:
    iter.toSeq() == @[T 1, 3]

  GC_fullCollect()
```

This test stopped failing after this workaround
https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/pull/849

**Environment:**
 - Nim Compiler Version 1.6.14
 - Tag/Commit v0.1.2:
 - Arch: amd64

title: Generalize reverse index mapping in EC strategies
body:
Although our erasure coding module would appear to accept any interleaving strategy (i.e. an arbitrary permutation) for input blocks, the [actual code that performs the reverse mapping](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/master/codex/erasure/erasure.nim#L85) of block indices into positions in the encoding array is specific to the stepped strategy. 

This means that, currently, attempting to use any other strategy, including the linear strategy, will result in breakage. Indeed, the reverse mapping $f(i, s)$ for an index $i$ under the linear strategy using $s$ encoding steps should be:

$$
f(i,s) = \left\lfloor \frac{i}{s} \right\rfloor
$$

In general, reverse mapping should be a property of the strategy itself, and the erasure coding module should contain no code that performs it.

### Other pieces with hard-coded index mapping

* erasure code [decode](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/master/codex/erasure/erasure.nim#L412) function

title: Add support for finishing iteration prematurely in `AsyncIter` and `Iter`
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

`AsyncIter[T]` and is used for iterating through the results of db query. Db query needs disposing after it's no longer used. This is handled by this proc https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/6e9bdf1d7e9448038de2a0cf9ceaa9bdb7e49396/codex/stores/queryiterhelper.nim#L11

It handles disposing when there's either error occurred at some point or when query iterator got drained (all item were consumed without error).

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Sometimes we would like to finish iterating prematurely (without consuming all items from query). Example of such use case is here
https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/6e9bdf1d7e9448038de2a0cf9ceaa9bdb7e49396/codex/sales/reservations.nim#L605

We're iterating through items to find an item of our iterest and then we stop iterating. Such use case needs disposing a query iter. Currently this use case cannot be covered with `AsyncIter[T]`

**Additional context**

Auto disposal may be done with GC hooks once we'll use ARC. It was already mentioned by @benbierens in this TODO
https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/6e9bdf1d7e9448038de2a0cf9ceaa9bdb7e49396/codex/sales/reservations.nim#L629

body:
@tbekas and I discussed adding a `forEach` or `collect` macro to the async-iterators. Currently the codebase uses a lot of regular `for` loops and it's easy to not correctly call `await`, especially when using `items` iterator.

A `forEach` or `forAwait` would enable using AsyncIterator's more naturally and safely.

title: Discuss & plan moving to async-io
body:
@tbekas and myself were discussing the issue of blocking disk io in the client. This impacts both file and sqlite access. We'd put moving to a multithreaded io until after the testnet release.

Before working on this we need to have a couple of client team meetings specifically to discuss what and how to do this. Ideally we can share perspectives and get agreements on approaches and how to break it up and plan things.

Suggested points to discuss:

- multi-thread datastore vs multithread file-ops
- multi-threading sqlite
- implications for datastore `update` operation
- data models, specifically
  + using seqs vs SharedPtr types
  + implications refc vs orc, copying overhead

title: Benchmark suite for proof circuits
body:
Proof circuits work on a set number of chunks of given size, both of which are parameters that can be tweaked. We'd like to put together a benchmarking suite that allows us to understand how fast they work when generating and verifying proofs with various settings for these parameters.

Benchmarking entails a number of steps (download ceremony files, setup procedures, run actual benchmark) that should be automated as part of this task. The outcome should be a set of scripts that allows one to input different parameters and get performance statistics regarding proving and verification times for said parameters.

Starting point are the existing tests for [proof generation](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/master/tests/codex/slots/sampler/testsampler.nim#L134) and [verification](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/master/tests/codex/slots/sampler/testsampler.nim#L161). The code for generating circuits is in the [codex-storage-proofs-circuits](https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-storage-proofs-circuits/) repo.

title: enabling hole punching and other libp2p goodies
Marketplace fillSlot reverts for unknown reason
body:
Found during marketplace dist-tests using Geth. Scenario:
(Using [...] to indicate a range of values for which tests have been run to check that they have no influence on this issue.)

1. 1x Host node with 11GB of quota and initial balance of 100000.
2. Host creates availability with:
    size: 10GB
    maxDuration: 30mins
    minPrice:1
    maxCollateral: 20
3. 1x Client node with initial balance of 234.
4. Client uploads a file of size [512KB - 10MB].
5. Client creates request with:
    pricePerSlotPerSecond: 2
    requiredCollateral: 10
    numberOfNodes: [3 - 8]
    tolerance: (nodes / 2)
    duration: [5mins - 20 mins]
    expiry: (duration - 1min)

6. Expect contract to reach "Started" state before expiry + grace period.

This test fails unreliably. (aprox. 4/5 runs fail. 1/5 pass.)
The failing runs indicate that the "slotFilled" onChain event is not raised often enough. For example:
In a run with 5 slots, the slotFilled event will occur 4 times. By adding a confirm(0) call to `contracts/market.nim`, we were able to detect that the invocation of 'fillSlot' fails with error message: "Transaction reverted with unknown reason".

title: Delay Codex startup when GethNode is syncing
body:
When Codex starts before the connected Geth node is fully synced, it may happen that Codex-contracts are not yet available. This can result in an incorrect initialization of the marketplace logic.

To solve this: Delay Codex at start-up until the Geth node is synced.
To do this, the isSync information must be added to nim-ethers.

The point of this change is to give testnet users a much easier time: They won't need to start and restart codex manually once their Geth is up-to-date.

title: Improvements to want list management and block transmission
body:
Problem
======

Peers in Codex keep local context information on wantlists for neighboring peers (i.e., peers with which it is actively maintaining a libp2p connection). To ease discussion, we refer to the peer keeping the local context information as `peer`, and its neighboring peers as `neighbor`s. 

Currently, whenever a new block is received by the peer, it will scan its local context and:

1. figure out which neighbors want the block (i.e. have the block in their wantlist);
2. enqueue a [a send task](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/master/codex/blockexchange/engine/engine.nim#L519) for that neighbor.

This send task will then _i)_ [take the wantlist for the neighbor](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/master/codex/blockexchange/engine/engine.nim#L529); _ii)_ [look up each one of the blocks contained in it](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/master/codex/blockexchange/engine/engine.nim#L552); _iii)_ [send those blocks over the network to the neighbor](https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/blob/master/codex/blockexchange/engine/engine.nim#L562). 

This simple strategy currently has the following shortcomings:

1. local wantlists are allowed to grow without bounds. This may lead to excessive memory usage at the peer or, worse, extremely long lookup times as tasks to lookup blocks for a neighbor's wantlist are run. This is especially true if active cancellation of want-haves fails to whatever reason, and the peer is left with large numbers of stale wantlist entries;
2. the peer currently does not cap the amount of blocks it tries to send, which may lead to increased latencies and unfairness at times of higher load.

Improvement Sketch
================

**Bounded wantlists.** Local wantlists need to be bounded, possibly by means of an LRU strategy. This means neighboring peers may need to re-transmit their want-haves to make sure they are not lost due to eviction, and may also need to select which blocks to request to optimize scheduling (i.e. if I want more blocks than the maximum size of a local wantlist, then spreading such request amongst several peers may be a good idea).

**Bounded sends.** Capping the amount of blocks sent per run of the send task can allow better fairness and lower latencies. The idea is that the send task will send at most `k` blocks per run, and another send task will be enqueued for the peer should the total number of blocks that needs to be sent exceed `k`.

**Tagging to avoid double lookup.** Local wantlist entries can be tagged so that individual blocks are not looked up more than once per peer. Such tags need then be invalidated once the actual block is received by the peer.

**Disconnect on active cancellation.** Active cancellation should mitigate stale entries, but it may fail. Issuing disconnects on failed sends for cancellation messages will force neighbors and peer to wipe their local contexts, thus naturally keeping wantlist sizes at a more manageable size.

title: Improvement: Async iterator template
body:
The async-iterator pattern is repeating in the codebase and it is just begging to be turned into a nice friendly reusable template or macro or whatever.
Declarations:
```blockstore.nim
  GetNext* = proc(): Future[?Cid] {.upraises: [], gcsafe, closure.}

  BlocksIter* = ref object
    finished*: bool
    next*: GetNext
```
Iterator:
```
iterator items*(self: BlocksIter): Future[?Cid] =
  while not self.finished:
    yield self.next()
```
Probably a big part of the implementation of methods like `listBlocks` can also be extracted.

title: Implement new proving scheme using 2D erasure coding

labels.name: epic
labels.description: Tracks a yearly team epic (only for codex-storage/codex-pm repo)

body:
Attempts to detail out the work for integrating [a new design][2] for storage
proofs into the codebase.

Flows
-----

The following flows are affected by the new storage proof scheme.

#### Requesting storage ####

1. Erasure code for distribution
    - generate K + M slots that will be distributed over K + M hosts
2. Erasure code each slot to prepare for storage proofs
    - encode in 2 dimensions
    - calculate Merkle root using SNARK friendly hash function
3. Post request on chain
    - include Merkle root for each slot

#### Host downloading slot data ####
1. Download slot data for the slot we're trying to fill
2. Erasure code slot data to prepare for storage proofs
    - encode in 2 dimensions
    - calculate Merkle tree, store upper levels on disk
    - check that calculated Merkle root matches the storage request

#### Host creating storage proofs ####
1. Get random seed from block hash
2. Calculate shard indices from random seed
3. Hash shards
4. Calculate Merkle inclusion paths for shards
5. Execute SNARK to generate proof

#### Checking of storage proofs ####
1. Verify SNARK proof on-chain

Expected work
-------------

Hashing function
- [x] Implement library for Poseidon2 (or something better) in Nim ([initial implementation][4])

Merkle trees for storage proofs
- [ ] Calculate Merkle tree with Poseidon2
- [ ] Serialize / deserialize Merkle tree up to a certain depth
- [ ] Calculate Merkle inclusion proofs

2D Erasure coding
- [ ] Implement encoder for 2D erasure coding
- [ ] Store one dimension on disk, calculate the other when needed

Update Marketplace Contracts
- [x] Remove old PoR related attributes from request and
       add Merkle root for storage proofs
    - https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-contracts-eth/pull/72
    - https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/pull/590
- [ ] Add SNARK verifier

SNARK
- [ ] Calculate shard indices from random seed
- [ ] Integrate into Nim
- [ ] Perform Perpetual Powers of Tau setup

Purchasing
- [ ] Call 2D erasure encoding function for each slot
- [ ] Call Merkle tree generation function for each slot
- [ ] Store Merkle root in request

Sales
- [ ] Download slot data only (https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/issues/557)
- [ ] Call 2D erasure encoding function
- [ ] Call Merkle tree generation function
- [ ] Save partial Merkle tree on disk
- [ ] Check Merkle root against request

Proving (now part of Sales)
- [ ] Calculate shard indices from random seed
- [ ] Recalculate shards that are not stored on disk
- [ ] Hash shards
- [ ] Read partial Merkle tree from disk
- [ ] Calculate Merkle inclusion paths for shards
- [ ] Execute SNARK to generate proof

Open Questions
--------------

- What do we do when we cannot generate a proof? Should we try to use local
  erasure decoding to fix some parts? How do we know which parts to fix?
- Do we need a decoder for the 2D erasure coding?

References
----------
- [Analysis of proving scheme][1]
- [Design of proving scheme][2]
- [Design of Merkle tree in manifest][3]

[1]: https://github.com/codex-storage/zk-research-artifacts/blob/master/sampling/sampling.pdf
[2]: https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-research/pull/184
[3]: https://github.com/codex-storage/codex-research/blob/master/design/metadata-overhead.md
[4]: https://github.com/codex-storage/zk-research-artifacts/tree/master/hashes/Poseidon2/Nim

title: Block Iteration Rework
body:
Prerequisite:
In order to work on this issue, the Merkelization efforts have to be finished or at least fairly mature.

Current situation:
The client iterates through all the blocks in the repostore, for two reasons: To advertise their CIDs to the network, and to detect and delete expired blocks.
The advertising loop runs every 30 minutes and iterates all blocks using `listBlocks` which uses directory-traversal in discovery.nim.
The maintenance runs every 10 minutes, iterates blocks in chunks of 1000 each time which uses the metadata-SQLite repository, in maintenance.nim.

These operations are capable of slowing down disc IO enough that in some unlucky cases, uploads and/or downloads will breach timeout limits and fail.

Proposed future situation:
Instead of maintenance and discovery each looping through the blocks in their own way, a single module should be dedicated to the iteration of all blocks. Maintenance and discovery should 'subscribe to' or use this module in some way to do their work.
This block-iteration module should use the SQLite metadata store to do its work, as that is likely much faster than directory-traversal.
This block-iteration module must be lockable: When an upload or download starts, iteration is locked until the operation is finished. This prevents block iteration from interfering with time-sensitive operations.
Currently, block iteration loops through ALL blocks, (manifests and datablocks alike) and does not distinguish between them. This isn't necessary: We can advertise and perform maintenance at the manifest/merkle-root level: We announce the merkle-roots + indices which we have available for download. And maintenance can be modified to only delete entire trees when they expire.
(In the further future: maintenance can be made more clever: deleting blocks only when available space is low and prioritizing the deletion of the least wanted/least profitable blocks first. For this reason, we may want to be able to perform maintenance on a block-level instead of a tree-level. Your input on this puzzle is welcome!)

title: Handle single block erasure coding (K=1)
body:
Erasure coding a dataset with a single block, K=1, is not currently supported:

> Currently we don't support erasure coding a single block, there has to be at least two blocks to be able to erasure code them.
>
> Leopard will not encode a single buffer, it will simply copy it back. Blocks are treated as a list of symbols and each block occupies one buffer. So, for all intents and purposes, when using one block, we're sending a vector of L*K elements, but K=1 in this case, so leopard simply copies it back.
>
> The options are:
>
> 1. Split the block into two and erasure code the two pieces together
> 2. Erasure code with a dummy block
> 3. ???

See https://github.com/codex-storage/nim-codex/pull/513 for more context.

title: Verify data availability upon restart of Sale state machine
body:
If a node has filled a slot and recovers from a catastrophic failure, on startup it will enter into the `Unknown` state where it will get the slot's state of `SlotState.Filled` from the chain. From there, it will it will go into the `SaleFilled` state then `SaleFinished`, where the slot is added to the proving mechanism. There is one problem though: the data for the slot has not been re-downloaded.

We need to be careful because we have made some assumptions about entering the `SaleDownloading` state: we assumed that this state would only be entered if we are starting a sales process in situ, as in we are not restoring the state from the chain.

title: Distributed Testing Frameworks
body:
# Distributed Testing Frameworks

For the purpose of testing Codex in real-world but still controlled environments, we've been looking into various frameworks for distributed testing. This document describes the results of the investigations and is expected to change.

## Requirements for the testing framework
See: https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/350

1. Reproducability - We should have to ability to re-run tests and we should expect the same results.
1. Controllability - We should be able to add/remove/update tests easily. (Without first learning every detail about the testing framework.)
1. Scalability - We should be able to easily adjust the size of the network and the data used for testing.

## Container orchestration
A docker image of Codex is available here: https://hub.docker.com/r/thatbenbierens/nim-codex (to be relocated). The environment variables of the image make it possible to run the same image in any number of different configurations easily. Container orchestrators are solutions that can spin up images and manage running instances and their (network) environments. Docker-compose and Kubernetes are commonly used. Some testing frameworks use a container orchestrator internally to manage their instances. Others operate against a virtualization API like Docker directly.

## Investigation into existing frameworks
At this moment I find that none of the existing frameworks provide all the functionality we're looking for. Some of them provide some of the features, but lack others or are closed in a way that we can't (easily or at all?) bolt our own workarounds to them. Investigation is on-going.

### Testground
Url: https://github.com/testground/
Got hello-world to run: Yes.
Pros:
1. Designed for distributed systems.
1. Runtime agnostic.
Cons:
1. Test code will be heavily influenced for framework-specific reasons.
1. Fire-and-forget test approach. No clear end-of-test or pass-or-fail moment.
1. Designed for 'manual' data/result gathering. Draw your own conclusion.

### Kurtosis
Url: https://www.kurtosis.com
Got hello-world to run: Tried to run Waku tests, failed for unknown reason.
Pros:
1. Used by Waku
Cons:
1. Closed-source.
1. Does not (currently) support testing on multiple systems.
1. Locked into go and star language/ecosystem.

### ClusterTest
Url: https://github.com/guseggert/clustertest
Got hello-world to run: Yes
Pros:
1. Simple
Cons:
1. Does not support testing on multiple systems. (Local or AWS EC2)
1. Does not currently support container environment variables, also has restrictions on container lifecycle.
1. Not designed for containerized services.
1. Locked into go language/ecosystem.

## Proposed approach
### Coding against the Kubernetes API
Url: https://github.com/kubernetes-client/csharp
Got hello-world to run: Yes
Pros:
1. Kubernetes can orchestrate containers locally and distributed across hosts/cloud providers.
1. Flexibility to choose and switch machine/host/cloud providers.
1. Industry standard, unlikely to break under heavy load.
1. Serhan brings the experience
Cons:
1. Non-trivial setup and configuration for large and/or remote clusters.
1. We need to code the test framework ourselves

title: Unsuccessful uploads may pollute local blockstore with orphaned blocks
Cleanup API and extend its expressiveness
body:
Going through the API as part of documenting it for OpenAPI and have several suggestions on how to make it cleaner.

1. Use `json` for most, if not all, responses to have them unified
	-  `/connect` returns a simple string for all the responses
2.  Expanding expressiveness of our API using a more comprehensive range of HTTP status codes etc
 	- `/connect` could return `404` when a peer is not found
3. Validate data input and provide proper error messages
 	- `POST /sales`, for example, accepts a normal number instead of the required hex-decimal string for values and creates a Request with all values zeroed. Instead, it should properly error out with status 400 and a description of what is wrong.
 	- I would suggest creating a structure for error responses in the form of JSON with properties message and code, where a message would be a user-friendly verbose message and code would be a short string for API consumers to do assertions on similarly like NodeJS defines it: https://nodejs.org/api/errors.html#errorcode
4. Correct path semantic
	- `POST /storage/request/{cid}` passes CID **parameter** of the request as a path section. I would argue that it should be better to use a query parameter or move it directly into the JSON body. Moreover, there is `GET /storage/purchases/{id}`, which has a similar format, but the ID is a Request ID instead of a CID, which is quite confusing.
5. Proper and easy-to-use input types
	- I would suggest moving from the hexadecimal string for BigInts to using simply decimal string (for numeric values). It is very cumbersome to work with the API, where I have to convert all the inputs into hex numbers.
	- For some properties, the hex strings are not even needed, like for Storage Ask's `proofProbability` which should not be big int anyhow if I understand its usage correctly.

title: Wire up Memory cache

An in-memory cache implementing either 'datastore' or 'blockstore' abstraction can help improve performance for slow storage devices. 
Memory cache should use a least-recently-used approach.
Memory cache size should be adjustable from config.
Memory cache feature should be able to be disabled from config.

Consider adjusting current 'CacheStore'.

title: Enable `--styleCheck` for nim compilation
