title: Distributed Testing High Level Requirements and Roadmap
body:
# Distributed Testing High Level Requirements and Roadmap

## Background

As Codex approaches its MVP (Minimal Viable Product) release, stability and reliability as well as scalability and security become critical and require a more focused effort. This issue attempts to capture the requirements to get the current PoC implementation to it's desired MVP state.

## Requirements

The Codex protocol consists of several main components which needs to be tested in isolation as well as in concert. These are:

- The p2p related pieces
- The marketplace and consensus related pieces
- The remote auditing related pieces

For each of these we want:

- To test correctness, stability and scalability.
- Simulate different network and load conditions.
  - Ability to control the network topology and conditions (throttling, packet-loss, etc...)
- Collect and inspect metrics and logs in real time and post factum.
- Reproduce/rerun each specific configuration and test at will.
- Ability to run on remote elastic infrastructure (AWS, etc...) as well as locally.
  - We want to scale the number of nodes according to our needs.
  - Possibly run with and without containerization.

## Testing Approaches

There are two main testing approaches that we're pursuing:

- Blackbox (distributed) testing
  - These rely on distributed testing frameworks such as
    - [testground](https://github.com/testground/)
    - [kurtosis](https://www.kurtosis.com/)
    - [clustertest](https://github.com/guseggert/clustertest)
    - [beekeeper](https://github.com/ethersphere/beekeeper)
- Whitebox (testnets) testing
  - These relies on user testing and requires supporting infrastructure to spin up both internal and external testnets on an as needed basis

I believe that the testnet requirements are relatively simple, both in terms of infrastructure as well as setup complexity. Once there is a baseline configuration it can be reused many times, with minimal changes, accross different iterations of the testnet. In terms of infrastructure, the requirements seem relatively low - it should support a basaline number of nodes (10, 20, 30?), out of which, a few would be bootstrap nodes, with mildly higher requirements.

The distributed testing approach however is more involved and requires a lot more effort, both in the initial setup phases as well as in subsequent test cases/scenarios implementation and maintenance. For an example of similar efforts take a look at this projects:

- https://github.com/celestiaorg/test-infra - testground
- https://github.com/logos-co/wakurtosis - kurtosis
- https://github.com/guseggert/clustertest-kubo - clustertest
- https://github.com/ethersphere/beekeeper - beekeeper

## Current status

We have done some preliminary assessment of the above tooling and the possible approaches, however, no conclusive answer on which is best for our usecase or even if one is available.

Some preliminary requirements and results have been captured accross several issues, PRs and repositories:

- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/345
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/213
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/207
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/191
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/pull/255
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/140
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/173
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/172
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/174
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/281
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-codex/issues/279
- https://github.com/Shorssaud/network-testing-codex

## Next steps

We are currently sourcing two dedicated resources to help us move this effort forward - a [distributed testing engineer](https://jobs.status.im/?gh_jid=4872413), to assist with testing and a [devops engineer](https://jobs.status.im/?gh_jid=4864237), to bootstrap and manage the required infrastructure - come work with us if you like the challenge ;-).

As it currently stands, we need to understand the following:

1. [ ] What are our infrastructure requirements for the testnets and the distributed tests?
2. [ ] Understand and document testing framework and approach suits us best?
   1. [ ] What features are supported out of the box, which are planed and the expected timeline?
   2. [ ] Assess the need of an in house solution or major external contribution to an existing project?
3. [ ] Define and document initial testing scenarios and their respective requirements - see (1)

title: Performance evaluation of reference & Neptune implementations
minprotobuf limits message size to 4mb
body:
TL;DR

- minprotobuf is limited to 4mb message sizes
- manifests can hold up to ~131072 sha256  hashes
- largest file size that we can represent with a 4mb manifest is roughly ~8.5GB
- we can fix this with nested manifests
- it would be nice to set a more reasonable limit on manifest sizes - say ~10MB
- this needs allowing to specify/increasing the max size of the message in minprotobuf
- this would be needed anyway when exporting a manifest - even with nested manifests, you'd need to store all the manifest files together when exporting/importing

[minprotobuf](https://github.com/status-im/nim-libp2p/blob/unstable/libp2p/protobuf/minprotobuf.nim#L23) imposes a `4mb` size limit on messages. 

There isn't a limit that the standard imposes, but it largely makes sense to keep messages around a couple of megabytes as per the protobuf authors advice - https://stackoverflow.com/questions/34128872/google-protobuf-maximum-size. However, there are some legit use cases that might require larger message sizes, in our case, the `manifest` size grows proportionally to the block size and total dataset size and it could reach sizes of several dozens of megabytes. We have ways to mitigate this with nested manifests, but this needs to be implemented on the other hand, it would be nice if minprotobuf allowed to specify the max message size.

title: Hierarchical Manifests
body:
Manifests for larger datasets are too large. In order to solve that, we can implement hierarchical manifests. Simplest possible approach is to have two levels - root manifest contains CIDs of group manifests, which in turn contains CIDs of individual blocks. Group manifest should include 2^n blocks in order to allow recursive computation of Merkle tree hash.

It solves two problems:
- Managing large manifests. 1 GB manifest is awkward to write/read to BlockStore, send over network and just keep in memory.
- Advertising blocks available at the node. Advertising each 64 KB block is too much work, while advertising an entire dataset is too little discretion. If cache node can fetch data of one subblock (say, 64 MB) and then advertise their presence, it will be fast but don't flood the network with too many Presence messages.

title: Prefetching data streams

labels.name: epic
labels.description: Tracks a yearly team epic (only for codex-storage/codex-pm repo)

body:
This is general topic for discussion of situations where streaming can be made faster by prefetching stream data, especially from network peers.

node.retrieve has two b/g jobs - erasureJob and fetchBlocksJob which can be made more accurate by decoding/prefetching ahead fixed amount of blocks rather than the entire file at once.

erasureJob looks especially unreliable - first we need enough place to store all decoded data, otherwise this approach will fail. Second - it will require a lot of time, so reading from LPStream may fail for timeout.

For erasure decoding, we probably want a more intelligent downloader that fetches in recovery groups and kicks in recovery as soon as it got any K pieces. If those are the original (systematic) pieces then we're don't do anything, if they aren't the original we kick in recovery and continue downloading in parallel, then we either stop the recovery or the download, whichever succeeds first.

title: Process CancelledError
body:
CancelledError comes from chronos, it is raised when you Cancel a future. 

### Make it a template

CancelledError should be reraised each time we catch other CatchableError. I think we may have a template for that boilerplate:
```Nim
proc something {.async.} =
  await sleepAsync(10.minutes) # will raise CancelledError when `someFut.cancel()` is called
  # which will bubble up to `otherthing`

proc otherthing {.async.} =
  try:
    await something() # will raise CancelledError since `something` didn't handle it
  except CancelledError as exc:
    # If we want to catch CatchableError, we have to make sure to re-raise CancelledError first
    # otherwise, CancelledError may be swallowed
    raise exc
  except CatchableError as exc:
    discard

proc other {.async.} =
  let someFut = otherthing() # not awaited yet
  someFut.cancel()
  await someFut # will raise CancelledError
```

### Fix OnBlock type

Definition in blockstore.nim and its use in discovery.nim are incompatible in the exception list:

```Nim
# blockstore.nim:
  OnBlock* = proc(cid: Cid): Future[void] {.upraises: [], gcsafe.}

# discovery.nim:
  proc onBlock(cid: Cid) {.async.} =
    ...
    except CancelledError as exc:
      raise exc
```

### Propagate exceptions

When a task is cancelled by an exception, it needs to signal that to its "father" task, which in turn should cancel all its "son" tasks and raise CancelledError for itself, freeing all resources held by this task subtree.

title: Offload long-running tasks to other OS threads
body:
Here I save ideas from our discussion on subj. There are 3 things we should implement:
- thread pool for CPU-heavy tasks
- thread pool for I/O tasks (in particular, synchronous calls to OS)
- how to integrate them into the Chronos async eventloop

Resources:
- [Multithreading flavors: Choosing the right scheduler for the right job
](https://nim-lang.org/blog/2021/02/26/multithreading-flavors.html)
- https://github.com/mratsim/weave
- https://github.com/status-im/nim-taskpools
- https://github.com/yglukhov/asyncthreadpool/


### Communication

Since Chronos and task-executing threads are, indeed, run in different threads, we need communication channels that can work over thread bounds.

We have several candidates:
- [system/channels_builtin](https://nim-lang.org/docs/channels_builtin.html)
- [SPMC queue](https://github.com/status-im/nim-taskpools/blob/stable/taskpools/chase_lev_deques.nim) for sending data from Chronos to threadpools

Depending on compilation options, we may need to pass between threads only non-GCed objects, solutions are:
- [Isolated[T]](https://github.com/nim-lang/RFCs/issues/244)
- serialization
- gcUnref+gcRef in another thread
- allocShared, i.e. manual memory management
- read data into raw buffers and deserialize them in receiver thread

In order to identify tasks we may send with each task a random-generated id and return this id with the task answer.


### Thread pool for CPU-heavy tasks

It can be implemented by nim-taskpools, Weave or internally:
```Nim
proc cpuThread(pool: CPUThreadPool) = 
  while pool.notFinished:
    (id,task) = pool.taskQueue.recv()
    let res = task()
    pool.resultsQueue.send(id,res)
```


### Thread pool for I/O tasks

It can be implemented internally. The main difference compared to CPU-heavy taskpool is that we can run a lot of threads and create threads on demand:

```Nim
proc ioThread(pool: IOThreadPool) = 
  while pool.notFinished:
    (id,task) = pool.taskQueue.recv()
    if (pool.freeTasks-=1) == 0 && pool.runningTasks <= 100:
      pool.runningTasks += 1
      startThread(ioThread, pool)
    let res = task()
    pool.freeTasks += 1
    pool.resultsQueue.send(id,res)
```

### Integrate them into the Chronos async eventloop

This code:
```Nim
await ioOperation()
step = 1
```
is translated by `async` macro into closure iterator with code:

```Nim
let fut = ioOperation()
yield fut
step = 1
```
that's compiled by Nim into sort of:

```Nim
let fut = ioOperation()
fut.then:
  step = 1
return fut
```

So, when ioOperation will be finished, the future will complete and run its continuation that holds all the code after the `await ioOperation()` line.

WIP: code sample
```Nim
var ctx

var table: Table[threadid, Future[T]]
proc threadPoll() =
  for fut in ctx.futures:
    let flow = spawn fut.task
    if flow.isReady:
      let val = flow.tryRecv()
      let future = val.fut
      future.complete(val.value)
```

- asyncqueue from Chronos
- channels from taskpool

title: A minimal payment channel implementation for chunk retrieval
body:
Goal: show that payment channels are a scalable solution for chunk payments.

A first version of payment channels has been added in #9. It implements a design based on Nitro state channels. It shows the viability of using payment channels, but it doesn't show that payment channels can be opened and closed fast enough to use for bandwidth payments.

We'd like to add a scaling solution for this. So far we've identified two options during our research:
  1. use Nitro *virtual* channels as per the [draft RFC](https://github.com/status-im/dagger-research/pull/8), or
  2. use an async blockchain to open and close channels.

Depending on the outcome of the research in https://github.com/status-im/dagger-research/issues/28 and https://github.com/status-im/dagger-research/issues/33 we'll make a choice and implement it here.
